## Case 1

Variants list crashes when cancer tier key is int

To reproduce:
1. Add entry as custom term `{ "key": 6, "label": "Tier achtung", "description": "Wir haben nur ein stuhl", "label_class": "default" }`
2. Goto a cancer case
3. Classify as tier achtung
4. Goto variants list view

## Case 2

Variants list view crashes if an old evaluation term has been removed

To reproduce
1. Add a custom dismissal term to the database, `{ "key": 60, "label": "Our major issue", "description": "Major major issue", "evidence": ["pileup", "GT"] }`
2. Goto a variant for a case
3. Dismiss variant with the term "Our major issue"
4. Remove the dismissal term from the database
5. Go to case_name/variants

I would have thought satpy had other uses of tar that are more in need of this fix (in a Satpy reader), but this particular fix is about downloading a specific hardcoded dataset of demo data. We know what this data is.

> There does not seem to be an example of reading the buffer into a qcodes parameter. Is this supported? It is an esential feature for a qcodes driver to be able to capture the data as a parameter so it can be storred in a dataset easily

I have made `data` a parameter, and the usage is as following:
> buffer.data_start = 1
buffer.data_end = total_data_points
v=buffer.data()

the type of `v` is a list, with float-type elements.

User can specify several different elements, such as time, measurement, unit...
>buffer.elements(['measurement', 'time', 'measurement_unit'])
data=buffer.data()

In this case, the data will be a 3xN matrix/array, of which all the elements are str-type.

I didn't add the `vals=Arrays(...)` nor the `parameter_class`, for the `data` parameter, because:
1. the shape of the data is not fixed, it depends on how many elements the user wants;
2. the `parameter_class=...` part can only be added if there is the `vals=Arrays(..)`
suggestion? @jenshnielsen

Thanks @ericl for the examples. I looked into both cases, and I found the data compression ratio is very high, because data is encoded very well, and both data has dictionary encoding and RLE encoding.

```
>>> parquet_file = pq.ParquetFile('/tmp/text/fb28097980524bbb928d90f39fec278e_000019.parquet')
>>> parquet_file.metadata.row_group(0).column(0)
<pyarrow._parquet.ColumnChunkMetaData object at 0x7fc3a21da5e0>
  ...
  compression: SNAPPY
  encodings: ('PLAIN_DICTIONARY', 'PLAIN', 'RLE')
  has_dictionary_page: True
  dictionary_page_offset: 4
  data_page_offset: 75
  total_compressed_size: 2115
  total_uncompressed_size: 3061
  
>>> parquet_file = pq.ParquetFile('/tmp/tensor/a495e7499dc54cd4b26179ae4ef7d95f_000199.parquet')
>>> parquet_file.metadata.row_group(0).column(0)
<pyarrow._parquet.ColumnChunkMetaData object at 0x7fc3a21bb720>
  ...
  compression: SNAPPY
  encodings: ('PLAIN_DICTIONARY', 'PLAIN', 'RLE')
  has_dictionary_page: True
  dictionary_page_offset: 4
  data_page_offset: 20047
  total_compressed_size: 41998
  total_uncompressed_size: 85692
```

Decoding strategy is determined dynamically during Parquet file write runtime. One row group can have multiple encoding as above (dictionary, RLE, plain), so it's very hard to reverse engineering in-memory data size based on columns statistics.

So I feel the sampling approach in this PR is good direction to go.

I am personally not a big fun of using dataclasses or attrs in a public library API, because

1. They add some startup overhead (probably negligible in this case though).
2. They add "hidden" APIs to the classes, like `dataclasses.asdict`, `dataclasses.replace` etc. which users will eventually rely on, making it part of the public API.
3. They somewhat reduce (or add friction to) the library's control should it ever need it.
4. They tend to do some funky stuff, for example in pytest we had some trouble with what dataclasses does [here](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/897387d2c8a956e74770c6bdd0447dfec61e8e27/Lib/dataclasses.py#L615).

That said, if you're OK with this, the code LGTM except one comment.

    sources = {
        "project/data.json": "",

        "project/html/hello.html": "",
        "project/__init__.py": "",
        "project/app.py": dedent(

            from pathlib import Path

            def resources_exists():
                try:
                    pkgutil.get_data("project.html", "hello.html")
                except Exception:
                    return False
                return Path("{tmpdir}/project/data.json").exists() 
            """
        ),
        "project/test_app.py": dedent(
            """\
            from project.app import resources_exists

            def test_resources_exists():
                assert resources_exists() is True
            """
        ),
        "project/BUILD": dedent(
            """\
            python_library(dependencies=[":html", ":json"])
            python_tests(name="tests")
            resources(name="html", sources=["html/*.html"])
            files(name="json", sources=["*.json"])
            """
        )
    }
    with setup_tmpdir(sources) as tmpdir:

    assert (

                Name                              Stmts   Miss  Cover

                {tmpdir}/project/__init__.py       0      0   100%
                {tmpdir}/project/app.py            8      2    75%

                TOTAL                                11      2    82%

        )
        in result.stderr

Hi @jgbradley1 and thanks a lot for this PR! Before you commit any more time into this, please note that we aren't sure yet if we want to add the WIDERFace dataset. We are currently in the process of adding [contributing guidelines](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/2663/) including new datasets. Right now our only metric is the number of citations:

- CelebA: [86](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?cites=286831505508630744)
- WIDERFace: [682](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?cites=8866787776930184069)

Given that WIDERFace seems to be used more than CelebA, I think it is reasonable to include it. That being said, I have no final say here. I suggest you hold further development until @fmassa gives green light for this. If he does I'm happy to review.

1 more questions & 2 more task:

Question: Do I understand it correctly that there's no way to use `YaraExpressionBuilder` together with newly introduced variables? From what I see, you can only initialize variables to some static values (strings, integers, floats, ...) but apart from that, you can't for example build `1 + 2` using builder interface. It's something that should be improved but we don't have a use-case for it right now so we'll be fine without it.

Task 1: Variables and iterables are going to be Avast-only feature for some time (until upstream decides what to do with our changes) so I would expect that these will be enabled only when Avast-mode is enabled. When you are creating new `Yaramod` instance, you are able to provide it something called `ImportFeatures`. I would rename this to `Features` and also include grammar modifications in there.

Task 2: Please also add documentation for these new features in `docs/rtd`. Both building and parsing rulesets. Don't forget to also change references to `ImportFeatures` and mentioning them in description of these new features.

This is an interesting change.

Overall the change (returning a proper exception to the client instead of a 500) makes absolute sense.

Unfortunately you ended up needing to do a bunch of surgery to the codebase to make it happen because raising an `HTTPexception` from within a `Request` requires that `Request` reference `HTTPException`, but `ExceptionMiddleware` was in the same module as `HTTPException`, which caused circular references. This makes the diff super ugly and huge relative to the small straightforward feature.

But I think that's okay: we are paying the price to fix an issue that already existed in our codebase that might have caused us issues down the road some other time. And personally I think moving the middleware into `middlewares/` really cleans things up nicely.

I'm approving this, I do however want 1 more approver and some consensus on the deprecation policy because this is a breaking change for our downstream dependencies.

Hello @thedodd.

I agree, that the `static_primary` feature is useful and there is a certain demand for it from the community - I've been asked many times about the possibility to run clusters with Patroni, but without automatic failover, because people often want to simplify configuration management.

Right now the feature is very limited, and supports only single-node clusters, but I don't see any problem that it could be extended to support clusters of arbitrary size:
- Node that thinks that is it configured as static_primary should continue running as primary (or promote) even if DCS is down/not accessible or updated_lock()/acquire_lock() calls have failed.
- Other nodes should always use the static_primary instead of whatever is stored in the leader key in DCS.
- We can also support a switchover via Patroni REST API/patronictl. It will simply change the static_primary field in the config.
- If the static_primary value is changed in DCS, the new node should not promote until the old one is shut down cleanly. We can promote though if the old one isn't accessible.
- We need to document that in case of a network spilt it is the responsibility of the user to do STONITH of the isolated old primary. Such a situation could happen for example with Etcd, where we don't use quorum reads - the old static_primary will continue to see the stale data.

The addition of `close` is an interesting idea. Do you have a use case in mind? So far I've always used this for cases where there's some persistent data and subscribers are waiting for it to change, so "closing" isn't very intuitive -- the data might stop changing, but it's still there! Naming: naming is always hard, and especially for something like this, where there's no prior art (that I know of). But, it's important, since I think this is one of those cases where at first look the semantics are counterintuitive and hard to explain, even though they feel very natural once you wrap your head around them. So if the name can point people in the right direction, it'll help a lot.

"Stream" is maybe not ideal, because we use that as a term of art for byte streams. But the big thing I want is a name that hints at the coalescing, and the suitability for tracking persistent *state* rather than transient *events*.

`VersionTracker`? `UpdateNotifier`? `ChangeNotifier`? `Refresher`? `StateTracker`?

> I am unsure if children[1] is always guaranteed to be the operator.

The answer is no :(

Here's a few examples of where `parent.children[1].type == token.EQUAL` falls apart:

1. `test += (a := 1)` (who would write this tho?)
2. `a, b = (test := (1, 2))`
3. `test: int = (test2 := 2)`

edit: don't ask me how to workaround this problem, I have no idea

@avnishn Thanks a ton man. It's great that you made a working version of multi-gpu trainer. But this PR needs a good amount of work and re-design iteration until it's ready for production and aligned with the original design. I left some comments here and there for the immediate things that came to my mind. But more fundamentally, I think you need to take a step back and re-think about the structure of the abstractions and class hierarchies. Basically think about which parts are framework specific, which parts are not, How would a user know what to implement from the base class to be able to extend it and so on. You need to put these design decisions into a design doc so that we can discuss alternatives and whether we are leaking unwanted information to other places of the code-base.  Another question that you should be asking is how we make this code as simple as possible, and very easy to understand and follow. The principle is simple, you need to shard the data, run parallel fwd passes, run parallel backward passes and aggregate the result. Don't think that documentation will solve this problem, you need a better and simpler design with no information leakage between modules. Writing a documentation for this kind of code-base would be very easy.

> I was still mostly unconvinced that relying on get_queryset is really the right way to handle authorization, for the reasons around performance that you quoted from the Django docs, in that it tends to mean very expensive N+1 exponential SQL query fan-out.

For our use case, we have to rely on `get_queryset` to perform auth filtering at the DB level, as pulling all the data from the DB to Python to perform the filter would be a substantial performance/memory cost and would not scale. 

We're tackling the N+1 problem with dataloaders instead of `select_related`/`prefetch_related`. 


> Can I suggest that we update the "Global Filtering" graphene-django docs example to be more similar to that, where it doesn't actually change/filter the queryset, but instead just conditionally returns the queryset? I think it would also be worth noting in those docs something like "Warning: if you modify the queryset, such as performing any filtering here, it will result in N+1 queries when this DjangoObjectType is used as a relation from another Django model in graphene."

Updating the documentation is on my to-do for this PR indeed. Also to be noted that the N+1 issue occurs anyway if you use `graphene-django` without `select_related`/`prefetch_related`, so the comment should be more something like "Warning: modifying the queryset in `get_queryset` will cancel out any optimization technique such as `select_related`/`prefetch_related` in the model's manager (link to Django doc)"

> I know you mentioned previously that we should not infer the partition mapping, but I think it would be awkward if users are relying on our inferred partition mappings for execution (e.g. a user has a weekly partitioned asset with an upstream daily partitioned asset dependency, but is using the default partition mapping) but find that in this sensor we require the downstream asset to have an explicit partition mapping in order to find mapped partition keys. So for now, I've kept the current version of the code to use the default partition mapping if one isn't explicitly defined.

If asset A depends on asset B, but an explicit partition mapping wasn't provided for that dependency, I think using the default is definitely the right the to do: because that's the partition mapping that would be used for that dependency elsewhere.  The thing that I think is a little weird is that if A doesn't depend on B, we'd still return a partition mapping for it - in that case, I think of the partition mapping as undefined. @zoccoler, thank you very much for your work so far and pushing this forward. 

> Should we not try and broadcast mask to input array?

I don't quite understand how this would help. As I understand the issue, SciPy's QHull doesn't like that there is a dimension of length 1, fails and `convex_hull_image` returns an empty image without a surface. 

I'd rather prefer to have this edge case supported upstream, than code around it in our function. However, I'm really unsure if this is something that would get resolved by upstream in a reasonable time frame. The [error is raised in qhull](https://github.com/qhull/qhull/blob/c2ef2209c28dc61ccfd22514971236587e820121/src/libqhull/geom2.c#L1283-L1284) which is in turn wrapped by SciPy. And it's way easier on our side to check for a dimension of size 1 than do it once it's transformed to coordinates.

So in the meantime I've left a few comments; feel free to address them or wait for what SciPy says...

This works nicely: I've tested it with the following custom grouper designed to prioritise Landsat 8 > Landsat 5 > Landsat 7 geomedian, and it works great:

```
def _extract_time_from_ds(ds: Dataset) -> datetime.datetime:
    return normalise_dt(ds.center_time)

def sort_by_platform(ds):
    order = {'LANDSAT_5': 2, 'LANDSAT_7': 3, 'LANDSAT_8': 1}
    return order[ds.metadata.platform]


platform_grouper = GroupBy(dimension='time',
                           group_by_func=_extract_time_from_ds,
                           units='seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00',
                           sort_key=sort_by_platform)
```

As expected, I now get correct dates returned in the output xarray:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17680388/126108503-170ac57b-1370-41c1-9b4c-3e0be0ce69c4.png)

And my output nicely prioritises the correct sensors (e.g. no Landsat 7 striping from 2013 onward where both Landsat 8 and Landsat 7 data available):

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17680388/126108595-ca67aab7-0bbb-4325-9e33-347f739b504a.png)

Although it happens to be the case that I only found out that NumPy is supposed to have two capital letters after "NumpyArray" was defined, I intentionally kept the class name that way because "NumPyArray" is too bumpy; it would be easy to forget. In that name, "Numpy" is thought of as one part and "Array" is another: more nested structures would be a distraction. (Note "ByteMaskedArray" and "BitMaskedArray" vs "UnmaskedArray". All of those capitalizations were intentional.)

On the other hand, I can be persuaded to rename `nplike.NumPy` and `nplike.CuPy` (both of them together). There isn't a connection between these and `ak.layout.NumpyArray`; the different capitalization schemes wouldn't mentally conflict. I can make that part of this PR so that you can check for "Numpy\b". They're always fully qualified:

```bash
% egrep -r 'Numpy\b' src
src/awkward/__init__.py:    raise ImportError("Numpy 1.13.1 or later required")
src/awkward/_connect/_numba/builder.py:numpy = ak.nplike.Numpy.instance()
src/awkward/_connect/_numba/layout.py:numpy = ak.nplike.Numpy.instance()
src/awkward/_connect/_jax/jax_utils.py:numpy = ak.nplike.Numpy.instance()
src/awkward/_connect/_autograd.py:numpy = ak.nplike.Numpy.instance()
src/awkward/partition.py:numpy = ak.nplike.Numpy.instance()
src/awkward/nplike.py:        return Numpy.instance()
src/awkward/nplike.py:class Numpy(NumpyLike):
src/awkward/operations/describe.py:numpy = ak.nplike.Numpy.instance()
src/awkward/operations/convert.py:numpy = ak.nplike.Numpy.instance()
src/awkward/highlevel.py:numpy = ak.nplike.Numpy.instance()
src/libawkward/array/NumpyArray.cpp:        std::string("Numpy format \"") + format_
src/libawkward/array/NumpyArray.cpp:            std::string("cannot convert Numpy format \"") + format_
% egrep -r 'Cupy\b' src
src/awkward/nplike.py:        return Cupy.instance()
src/awkward/nplike.py:class Cupy(NumpyLike):
src/awkward/operations/convert.py:    cupy = ak.nplike.Cupy.instance()
```

Playing around with this a bit, I recall why we did not enable this aggregate type - it can cause havoc on columns with many distinct string values.  I propose modifying this aggregation to limit the string length to ~280 characters (tweet length), by short circuiting the iteration over the `std::set` which accumulates the unique join-ed values [here](https://github.com/finos/perspective/blob/master/cpp/perspective/src/cpp/sparse_tree.cpp#L1020) when this character limit is reached.  `std::set` is ordered so this should still create deterministic results.
